name: Eiffel Tower
prompt: This iron lattice tower dominates the skyline of one of Europe's most visited capitals.
hints: In the capital city of France
On the banks of the Seine River
fun_fact: The tower grows about 15 cm taller in summer due to thermal expansion of the iron.

name: Mount Everest
prompt: The highest point on Earth, straddling the border between two nations.
fun_fact: Everest grows about 4mm per year as the Indian tectonic plate pushes into Asia.

name: Amazon River
prompt: The world's largest river by discharge, flowing through the planet's biggest rainforest.
hints: Empties into the Atlantic Ocean near the equator
fun_fact: The Amazon discharges 20% of all fresh water that enters the world's oceans.

name: Sahara Desert
prompt: The world's largest hot desert, covering most of North Africa.
hints: Stretches from the Atlantic to the Red Sea
fun_fact: The Sahara was green and fertile just 6,000 years ago due to a shift in Earth's orbit.

name: Great Barrier Reef
prompt: The world's largest coral reef system, visible from space.
hints: In the Coral Sea, near Queensland
fun_fact: It's the only living thing on Earth visible from space with the naked eye.

name: Colosseum
prompt: An ancient amphitheater that once hosted gladiatorial combat for 50,000 spectators.
fun_fact: The Colosseum could be flooded with water for mock naval battles called naumachiae.

name: Machu Picchu
prompt: A 15th-century Inca citadel perched high in the Andes Mountains.
fun_fact: Machu Picchu was built without mortar — its stones fit so precisely a knife blade cannot pass between them.

name: Tokyo
prompt: The world's most populous metropolitan area, blending ancient temples with neon-lit skyscrapers.
hints: An island nation in the Pacific
On Honshu island, facing Tokyo Bay
fun_fact: Tokyo was originally a small fishing village called Edo before becoming Japan's capital in 1869.

name: Sahel Region
prompt: A semi-arid transition zone between the Sahara and tropical savanna in West Africa.
hints: Stretches across West and Central Africa
South of the Sahara, north of the savanna
fun_fact: The Sahel is experiencing rapid desertification, losing about 48 km of land to desert annually.

name: Victoria Falls
prompt: One of the world's largest waterfalls, known locally as 'The Smoke That Thunders.'
hints: On the border of two countries
On the Zambezi River in southern Africa
fun_fact: Victoria Falls creates its own microclimate — a permanent rainforest sustained by the constant mist.

name: Angkor Wat
prompt: The world's largest religious monument, built in the 12th century as a Hindu temple.
fun_fact: Angkor Wat is the only temple in the world that faces west, toward the setting sun.

name: Serengeti
prompt: Africa's most famous wildlife ecosystem, home to the largest animal migration on Earth.
hints: In a landlocked country south of the equator
fun_fact: Over 1.5 million wildebeest migrate through the Serengeti each year in a circular route.

name: Fjords of Norway
prompt: Dramatic glacially carved inlets stretching along the rugged western coast of Scandinavia.
hints: Along the west coast of Norway
fun_fact: Norway's coastline, if straightened, would stretch more than halfway around the Earth.

name: Dead Sea
prompt: The lowest point on Earth's surface, so salty that nothing can sink in its waters.
hints: On the border of Jordan and Israel
fun_fact: The Dead Sea is shrinking by about 1 meter per year due to water diversion from the Jordan River.

name: Bora Bora
prompt: A volcanic island in the South Pacific, famous for its turquoise lagoon and overwater bungalows.
hints: Located in the South Pacific Ocean
fun_fact: Bora Bora's lagoon was formed by a coral reef growing around an ancient volcanic cone.

name: Patagonia
prompt: A vast windswept plateau at the southern tip of South America, shared by two nations.
hints: Located at the southern tip of South America
In Argentina and Chile, near Cape Horn
fun_fact: Patagonia contains the world's third largest freshwater reserve in its glaciers.

name: Petra
prompt: An ancient rose-red city carved directly into sandstone cliffs, hidden for centuries.
hints: In a desert country east of Israel
fun_fact: Petra was the capital of the Nabataean kingdom and had a sophisticated water system 2,000 years ago.

name: Kilimanjaro
prompt: Africa's highest peak, an isolated snow-capped volcano rising from the equatorial plains.
hints: In northeastern Tanzania, near the Kenyan border
fun_fact: Kilimanjaro has lost over 80% of its ice cap since 1912 due to climate change.

name: Galapagos Islands
prompt: A volcanic archipelago in the Pacific whose unique wildlife inspired Darwin's theory of evolution.
fun_fact: Marine iguanas on the Galapagos are the only lizards in the world that swim in the ocean.

name: Chernobyl
prompt: Site of the world's worst nuclear disaster in 1986, now a ghostly exclusion zone.
hints: In northern Ukraine, near the Belarus border
fun_fact: Wildlife has thrived in the Chernobyl exclusion zone since humans left, including wolves and wild horses.

name: Cappadocia
prompt: A surreal landscape of fairy chimneys and underground cities carved by ancient civilizations.
fun_fact: The underground cities of Cappadocia could shelter up to 20,000 people and their livestock.

name: Maldives
prompt: The world's lowest-lying country, a necklace of coral atolls in the Indian Ocean.
hints: South of India and Sri Lanka
Near the equator in the central Indian Ocean
fun_fact: The Maldives has an average elevation of just 1.5 meters — making it the most at-risk nation from sea level rise.

name: Iguazu Falls
prompt: A semicircular curtain of 275 waterfalls wider than Niagara, straddling two nations.
hints: On the border of two countries
Where Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay meet
fun_fact: Eleanor Roosevelt reportedly exclaimed 'Poor Niagara!' when she first saw Iguazu Falls.

name: Lake Baikal
prompt: The world's deepest and oldest lake, holding 20% of the planet's unfrozen fresh water.
fun_fact: Lake Baikal is so deep it could hold all of the water in the North American Great Lakes combined.

name: Santorini
prompt: A crescent-shaped volcanic island in the Aegean Sea, with iconic white-domed churches.
hints: Part of the Cyclades islands in Greece
fun_fact: Santorini's shape was formed by a massive volcanic eruption around 1600 BCE — possibly inspiring the Atlantis myth.

name: Svalbard
prompt: A remote Arctic archipelago where polar bears outnumber humans and the sun never sets in summer.
hints: Midway between Norway and the North Pole
fun_fact: It's illegal to leave the main settlement of Svalbard without a rifle due to polar bear encounters.

name: Mariana Trench
prompt: The deepest point on Earth, plunging nearly 11km below the surface of the ocean.
fun_fact: The pressure at the bottom of the Mariana Trench is over 1,000 times that at sea level.

name: Cairo
prompt: Africa's largest city, ancient capital of pharaohs and home to the last of the Seven Wonders.
fun_fact: Cairo's metro area is so large it would be the 10th most populous country in Africa if it were a nation.

name: Siberian Taiga
prompt: The world's largest forest, stretching across northern Russia and storing vast amounts of carbon.
hints: Across Siberia, from the Urals to the Pacific
fun_fact: The Siberian taiga contains more trees than all other forests on Earth combined.

name: Iceland
prompt: An island nation straddling two tectonic plates, where geysers, volcanoes, and glaciers coexist.
hints: Just south of the Arctic Circle
fun_fact: Iceland is the only country in the world that sits on both the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates.

name: Nile River
prompt: The world's longest river, flowing north through the Sahara to the Mediterranean Sea.
hints: Runs through Egypt and empties into the Mediterranean
fun_fact: Ancient Egyptians invented the first calendar to predict the Nile's annual floods.

name: Timbuktu
prompt: A legendary Saharan trading city once considered the edge of the known world.
hints: In Mali, near the Sahara Desert
fun_fact: Timbuktu's ancient libraries contain over 700,000 manuscripts dating back to the 12th century.

name: Pamukkale
prompt: Cascading white travertine terraces formed by calcium-rich hot springs, nicknamed 'Cotton Castle.'
fun_fact: The ancient Greco-Roman city of Hierapolis was built atop Pamukkale's hot springs for their healing properties.

name: Borneo
prompt: The world's third-largest island, home to ancient rainforests and wild orangutans.
fun_fact: Borneo's rainforest is older than the Amazon — it has been continuously forested for 140 million years.

name: Acropolis of Athens
prompt: Ancient hilltop citadel overlooking the birthplace of democracy, crowned by a marble temple.
fun_fact: The Parthenon has no curved lines — every surface is slightly curved to correct for optical illusions.

name: Sahara Atlas Mountains
prompt: A mountain range forming the northern edge of the Sahara Desert in North Africa.
fun_fact: The Atlas Mountains are geologically connected to the Alps — both formed when Africa collided with Europe.

name: Yellowstone
prompt: A supervolcanic caldera in the Rocky Mountains, home to geysers and the world's largest hot spring.
hints: In Wyoming, at the Rocky Mountains
fun_fact: Yellowstone sits atop a supervolcano that has erupted three times — each time in a mass extinction event.

name: Stonehenge
prompt: A prehistoric ring of massive standing stones on an English plain, aligned with solstice sunrises.
fun_fact: The largest stones at Stonehenge were transported 240 km from Wales — the equivalent of dragging them from New York to Philadelphia.

name: Gobi Desert
prompt: Asia's largest desert, a cold high-altitude plateau between the Himalayas and Siberia.
hints: North of the Himalayas, south of Siberia
fun_fact: The Gobi Desert is expanding by about 3,600 square km per year due to desertification.

name: Taipei 101
prompt: A bamboo-inspired supertall skyscraper that was the world's tallest building from 2004 to 2010.
hints: On an island off the coast of China
fun_fact: Taipei 101 has a 660-tonne steel pendulum inside to dampen earthquake vibrations.

name: Tikal
prompt: Ancient Mayan pyramids emerging from the jungle canopy of Central America.
hints: In the Petén rainforest of Guatemala
fun_fact: Tikal was one of the most powerful Maya kingdoms and may have had a population of 100,000 at its peak.

name: Easter Island
prompt: The world's most remote inhabited island, famous for its mysterious giant stone statues.
hints: Located in the South Pacific Ocean
The most isolated inhabited land on Earth
Belongs to Chile, far off the South American coast
fun_fact: The Easter Island statues (moai) have full bodies buried underground — most people only see their heads.

name: Great Wall of China
prompt: A vast fortification stretching thousands of kilometers across the mountains of northern China.
hints: In the most populous country on Earth
fun_fact: The Great Wall is not actually visible from space with the naked eye — that's a common myth.

name: Zhangjiajie
prompt: Towering sandstone pillar formations that inspired the floating mountains in the film Avatar.
fun_fact: One of the pillars in Zhangjiajie was officially renamed 'Avatar Hallelujah Mountain' after the film's release.

name: Congo River
prompt: The world's deepest river and Africa's second longest, draining the world's second-largest rainforest.
hints: In the Democratic Republic of Congo
fun_fact: The Congo River is the only major river in the world that crosses the equator twice.

name: Uluru
prompt: A massive sacred sandstone monolith rising from the flat red desert of the Australian outback.
hints: In the Northern Territory, in the outback
fun_fact: Uluru extends about 2.5 km underground — what you see is only the tip of a much larger rock formation.

name: Oymyakon
prompt: The coldest permanently inhabited place on Earth, where temperatures have dropped to -71°C.
fun_fact: In Oymyakon, spit freezes before it hits the ground, and cars must be kept running 24 hours or they won't restart.

name: Dallol
prompt: Earth's hottest inhabited place, a psychedelic hydrothermal field of acid pools and salt pillars.
hints: In the Danakil Depression in Ethiopia
fun_fact: Dallol sits 116 meters below sea level in one of the most geologically active places on Earth.

name: Rio de Janeiro
prompt: A coastal city famous for its carnival, Christ the Redeemer statue, and iconic Sugarloaf Mountain.
hints: On the Atlantic coast of Brazil
fun_fact: The name 'Rio de Janeiro' means 'January River' — Portuguese explorers thought the bay was a river mouth.

name: Namib Desert
prompt: The world's oldest desert, where towering red dunes meet the cold Atlantic Ocean.
hints: Along the Atlantic coast of Namibia
fun_fact: The Namib has been arid for at least 55 million years, making it the oldest desert on Earth.

name: Sydney Opera House
prompt: A sail-shaped performing arts center on a harbor that became one of the 20th century's iconic buildings.
hints: On the harbor of Australia's largest city
fun_fact: The Sydney Opera House's roof is made of over 1 million self-cleaning tiles that repel dirt and grime.

name: Niagara Falls
prompt: A trio of massive waterfalls on the border between the United States and Canada.
hints: On the border between the USA and Canada
Between Lake Erie and Lake Ontario
fun_fact: Niagara Falls is slowly moving upstream at about 30 cm per year due to erosion.

name: Phuket
prompt: Thailand's largest island, where limestone karsts rise from turquoise Andaman Sea waters.
hints: Off the western coast of Thailand
fun_fact: Phuket was an important tin mining hub for over 400 years before becoming a tourist destination.

name: Zambezi River
prompt: The fourth-longest river in Africa, whose lower section plunges over one of the world's greatest waterfalls.
fun_fact: The Zambezi is the only major African river flowing into the Indian Ocean through a delta.

name: Istanbul
prompt: The only city in the world that spans two continents, once capital of two great empires.
hints: Capital of both the Byzantine and Ottoman empires
fun_fact: Istanbul has been the world's largest city multiple times throughout history.

name: Wadi Rum
prompt: A vast red sandstone valley in the Arabian desert, nicknamed the Valley of the Moon.
hints: In a desert country east of Israel
fun_fact: Wadi Rum has been used as a film location for Mars in multiple Hollywood movies.

name: K2
prompt: The world's second-highest mountain, considered more dangerous than Everest to climb.
hints: On the border of Pakistan and China
fun_fact: K2 has never been climbed in winter — it remains the only 8,000m peak with a winter unclimbed status.

name: Lake Titicaca
prompt: The highest navigable lake in the world, sacred to the Inca as the birthplace of the sun.
hints: On the border of Peru and Bolivia
fun_fact: Lake Titicaca has floating islands made of totora reeds that have been inhabited for centuries.

name: Saharan Silver Ant habitat
prompt: The central Sahara, home to one of the fastest and most heat-resistant insects on Earth.
fun_fact: The Saharan silver ant can survive temperatures of 70°C and runs at 108 body lengths per second.

name: Panama Canal
prompt: An engineering marvel connecting two oceans, saving ships 12,000km of sailing around South America.
hints: In a narrow strip of land between two continents
In Panama, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific
fun_fact: Ships going through the Panama Canal from the Pacific to the Atlantic actually travel east, not west.

name: Mecca
prompt: The holiest city in Islam, birthplace of the Prophet Muhammad and destination of the annual Hajj.
hints: In western Saudi Arabia, near the Red Sea
fun_fact: Mecca receives about 2-3 million pilgrims during the annual Hajj — the world's largest human gathering.

name: Siberian permafrost
prompt: Vast frozen ground covering most of northern Russia, now thawing and releasing ancient greenhouse gases.
hints: Across the Arctic regions of Siberia
fun_fact: Thawing Siberian permafrost has revealed perfectly preserved woolly mammoths up to 40,000 years old.

name: Atacama Desert
prompt: The driest non-polar desert on Earth, where some weather stations have never recorded rain.
hints: In northern Chile, between the Andes and the Pacific
fun_fact: Some parts of the Atacama may not have seen rainfall in over 400 years.

name: Komodo Island
prompt: A remote Indonesian island home to the world's largest living lizard, a venomous prehistoric predator.
hints: In Indonesia, between Bali and Flores
fun_fact: Komodo dragons have a forked tongue to smell in stereo, tracking prey up to 9 km away.

name: Banff National Park
prompt: Canada's oldest national park, where turquoise glacial lakes reflect jagged Rocky Mountain peaks.
hints: In Alberta, in the Canadian Rockies
fun_fact: The turquoise color of Banff's lakes comes from glacial 'rock flour' suspended in the meltwater.

name: Tierra del Fuego
prompt: An archipelago at the southernmost tip of South America, the closest land to Antarctica.
hints: At the southern tip of the continent
Shared by Argentina and Chile, near Cape Horn
fun_fact: Tierra del Fuego means 'Land of Fire' — named by Magellan after the fires he saw lit by indigenous peoples.

name: Socotra Island
prompt: An alien-looking island in the Arabian Sea, home to the dragon's blood tree found nowhere else on Earth.
hints: Between the Gulf of Aden and the Indian Ocean
fun_fact: Socotra is so isolated that 37% of its plant species exist nowhere else on Earth.

name: Pompeii
prompt: A Roman city perfectly preserved in volcanic ash after a catastrophic eruption in 79 CE.
hints: Near Naples, in the shadow of Mount Vesuvius
fun_fact: Pompeii's population was estimated at 20,000 — about 2,000 bodies have been found, suggesting most escaped.

name: Fjord of Norway — Geirangerfjord
prompt: A UNESCO-listed Norwegian fjord so narrow that waterfall mist dampens passing cruise ships.
fun_fact: Viking ships navigated this exact fjord 1,200 years ago — the waters are still deep enough for cruise ships today.

name: Dubai
prompt: A futuristic desert city that rose from a fishing village to home of the world's tallest building in 50 years.
hints: On the Persian Gulf, in the UAE
fun_fact: Dubai's Burj Khalifa is so tall that you can watch the sunset twice — once at the base, then again from the top.

name: Redwood National Park
prompt: Ancient forests on the California coast where trees grow taller than any other living thing on Earth.
hints: On the West Coast of the United States
fun_fact: The tallest known tree on Earth, Hyperion, is a coastal redwood standing 115.9 meters tall.

name: Capricorn/Tropic crossing — Sahara
prompt: The invisible geographic line at 23.5°N that marks where the sun is directly overhead at summer solstice.
hints: The Tropic of Cancer, crossing Libya
fun_fact: The Tropic of Cancer is slowly drifting southward at about 15 meters per year.

name: Salar de Uyuni
prompt: The world's largest salt flat, which transforms into an enormous mirror after rain.
hints: In the Bolivian Altiplano, high in the Andes
fun_fact: Salar de Uyuni contains over half the world's known lithium reserves beneath the salt crust.

name: Great Rift Valley
prompt: A vast geological scar across East Africa where the continent is slowly being torn apart.
hints: Extends from the Middle East to Mozambique
fun_fact: In 10 million years, the Horn of Africa will separate from the continent and form a new ocean.

name: Amazon Rainforest
prompt: The world's largest tropical rainforest, producing 20% of the Earth's oxygen.
fun_fact: The Amazon rainforest creates its own weather — its trees release so much moisture they generate 'flying rivers' in the sky.

name: Angkor
prompt: The ruins of the largest pre-industrial city in history, hidden for centuries beneath the jungle.
fun_fact: At its peak, Angkor had a population of up to 1 million people — making it the largest city on Earth in the 12th century.

name: Namib Skeleton Coast
prompt: A fog-shrouded Atlantic coastline littered with shipwrecks and whale bones, called 'The Land God Made in Anger.'
hints: Along the Atlantic coast of Namibia
fun_fact: The Skeleton Coast's name comes from the whale and seal bones left by 19th-century hunters and the bleached remains of shipwrecked sailors.

name: Ha Long Bay
prompt: A UNESCO bay of thousands of limestone karst islands rising from emerald green waters.
hints: In Southeast Asia, on the South China Sea
fun_fact: Ha Long Bay has over 1,600 islands, many containing caves large enough to hold entire villages.

name: Suez Canal
prompt: A human-made waterway that connects the Mediterranean to the Red Sea, cutting 7,000km off the Europe-Asia route.
hints: Connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea
fun_fact: When the Ever Given container ship blocked the Suez Canal in 2021, it cost the global economy $9 billion per day.

name: Myanmar/Burma — Bagan
prompt: An ancient plain dotted with over 2,000 Buddhist temples and pagodas, built between the 9th and 13th centuries.
hints: In central Myanmar, on the Irrawaddy River
fun_fact: At its height, the Bagan empire built a new temple every single day for 230 years.

name: Greenland Ice Sheet
prompt: The world's second-largest ice body, covering 80% of the world's largest island.
hints: On Greenland, between the Arctic and Atlantic oceans
fun_fact: If the Greenland ice sheet melted entirely, global sea levels would rise by about 7 meters.

name: Matterhorn
prompt: A distinctive pyramid-shaped peak in the Alps, one of the most photographed mountains in the world.
hints: On the border of Switzerland and Italy
fun_fact: The Matterhorn was formed not by volcanic activity, but by glaciers that carved away softer rock over millions of years.

name: Okavango Delta
prompt: The world's largest inland delta, where a river ends in the Kalahari Desert rather than reaching the sea.
fun_fact: The Okavango River flows toward the sea but evaporates entirely in the Kalahari — it never reaches the ocean.

name: Aral Sea
prompt: Once the world's fourth-largest lake, now largely a desert after Soviet irrigation projects drained it.
fun_fact: Soviet engineers accidentally created a desert in 30 years — the Aral Sea lost 90% of its water between 1960 and 2000.

name: Waitomo Glowworm Caves
prompt: Limestone caves whose ceilings are illuminated by thousands of bioluminescent worms, resembling a starry sky.
hints: In New Zealand, on the North Island
fun_fact: The glowworms in Waitomo are actually larvae of a fungus gnat that spin silk threads to catch prey attracted by their light.

name: Maldives — North Male Atoll
prompt: A ring-shaped coral reef island where the highest point is barely above sea level.
hints: Near the equator, south of India
fun_fact: An atoll is formed on the rim of a sunken volcanic island — the mountain is gone, only the reef remains.

name: Lake Victoria
prompt: Africa's largest lake and the source of the White Nile, shared by three nations.
hints: On the equator in East Africa
Bordered by Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania
fun_fact: Lake Victoria was not known to Europeans until 1858, even though it's larger than the entire country of Ireland.

name: Lencois Maranhenses
prompt: A coastal desert in Brazil where thousands of freshwater lagoons fill among pure white sand dunes after the rains.
hints: Near the Atlantic coast of Brazil
fun_fact: The lagoons appear for just a few months each year when rainwater collects in dips between the dunes.

name: North Pole
prompt: The northernmost point on Earth, floating on sea ice in the middle of the Arctic Ocean.
hints: At the top of the Earth
fun_fact: There is no land at the North Pole — it sits on sea ice floating above an ocean about 4,000m deep.

name: South Pole
prompt: The southernmost point on Earth, located on a high polar plateau in the world's coldest continent.
hints: At the bottom of the Earth
fun_fact: The South Pole sits on about 2,835 meters of ice — if that ice melted, the pole would drop 900 meters.

name: Caspian Sea
prompt: The world's largest landlocked body of water, technically a lake despite its name and size.
hints: Bordered by Russia, Kazakhstan, Iran, Azerbaijan, and Turkmenistan
fun_fact: The Caspian Sea contains 90% of the world's wild beluga sturgeon, the source of the most expensive caviar.

name: Kerguelen Islands
prompt: One of the most remote inhabited archipelagos on Earth, a French territory with no permanent civilian population.
hints: Located in the southern Indian Ocean
Roughly equidistant from Africa, Australia, and Antarctica
fun_fact: The Kerguelen Islands are so remote that the only way to reach them is by a ship that makes the journey 4 times a year.

name: Mount Fuji
prompt: Japan's iconic snow-capped stratovolcano, the country's highest peak and a sacred symbol for centuries.
hints: On an island nation in the Pacific
On Honshu island, southwest of Tokyo
fun_fact: Mount Fuji is technically three overlapping volcanoes — the current cone was built on top of two older ones.

name: Denali
prompt: North America's highest peak, rising dramatically from the tundra plains of the far north.
hints: In Alaska, in the Alaska Range
fun_fact: Denali has the greatest vertical rise from base to summit of any mountain on Earth — greater even than Everest.

name: Pangong Lake
prompt: A high-altitude Himalayan lake that changes colors throughout the day from blue to green to red.
hints: On the border of India (Ladakh) and China (Tibet)
fun_fact: Pangong Lake sits at 4,350 meters elevation — higher than any mountain in Europe.

name: Lake Malawi
prompt: Africa's third-largest lake, home to more fish species than any other lake on Earth.
hints: Bordered by Malawi, Tanzania, and Mozambique
fun_fact: Lake Malawi contains more species of fish than all of Europe's lakes and rivers combined.

name: Siberia — Lena Pillars
prompt: Towering stone columns along a Siberian river, formed by millennia of frost and erosion.
hints: Along the Lena River in Yakutia, Siberia
fun_fact: The Lena Pillars formed about 540 million years ago when this land was at the bottom of a shallow sea.

name: Dolomites
prompt: A dramatic range of pale rock spires in the Italian Alps, glowing pink and orange at sunset.
hints: In the Alps, near the Austrian border
fun_fact: The Dolomites were once a coral reef at the bottom of a tropical sea — 250 million years ago, this area was near the equator.

name: Kerala Backwaters
prompt: A network of lagoons, lakes, and canals running parallel to the Arabian Sea coast of southern India.
hints: On the southwestern coast of India
fun_fact: The Kerala backwaters network is over 900 km long — nearly the distance from Paris to Warsaw.

name: Torres del Paine
prompt: Granite towers rising dramatically from the Patagonian steppe at the end of the world.
fun_fact: The Torres del Paine towers were formed by magma that solidified underground and was later exposed by glacial erosion.

name: Angkor Thom
prompt: The last great capital of the Khmer Empire, a walled city of temples and royal palaces.
hints: In northwestern Cambodia, next to Angkor Wat
fun_fact: Angkor Thom's walls are 8 meters high and 12 km long, enclosing an area larger than modern-day Paris.

name: Black Sea
prompt: An inland sea between Europe and Asia, almost entirely surrounded by land with only a narrow connection to the Mediterranean.
hints: Connected to the Mediterranean through the Bosphorus
fun_fact: The Black Sea's deep waters have no oxygen — ships that sank 2,000 years ago are still perfectly preserved on the seafloor.

name: Zhangye Danxia
prompt: Layered multicolored rock formations that look like a painting, created by mineral deposits over 24 million years.
hints: In Gansu Province, near the Silk Road
fun_fact: The colors in Zhangye Danxia are natural — red from iron oxide, green from chlorite, and yellow from limonite.

name: Ngorongoro Crater
prompt: The world's largest inactive volcanic caldera, a natural enclosure for Africa's densest wildlife.
hints: In northern Tanzania, west of Kilimanjaro
fun_fact: The Ngorongoro Crater has no need for fences — the steep walls naturally keep most wildlife inside.

name: Hagia Sophia
prompt: A 1,500-year-old domed cathedral that became a mosque, then a museum, then a mosque again.
hints: In a city spanning two continents
fun_fact: The Hagia Sophia's dome was the largest in the world for nearly 1,000 years.

name: Sundarbans
prompt: The world's largest mangrove forest, home to Bengal tigers that have learned to swim between islands.
hints: In the Bay of Bengal delta
Where the Ganges meets the sea, in Bangladesh and India
fun_fact: The Sundarbans tigers are the only tigers in the world that regularly swim in saltwater.

name: Faroe Islands
prompt: A remote North Atlantic archipelago of dramatic sea cliffs and grass-roofed villages.
fun_fact: The Faroe Islands have more sheep than people — about 80,000 sheep for 50,000 humans.

name: Saharan Tuareg territory
prompt: The heartland of the Tuareg, the 'Blue People' of the Sahara, nomadic keepers of ancient trade routes.
fun_fact: The Tuareg call themselves 'Kel Tamasheq,' meaning 'People of Tamasheq,' and have never been permanently conquered.

name: Grand Canyon
prompt: A colossal gorge carved over millions of years by a river through layers of ancient rock.
hints: In Arizona, carved by the Colorado River
fun_fact: The oldest rocks at the bottom of the Grand Canyon are nearly 2 billion years old — almost half the age of Earth.

name: Bali
prompt: A Hindu-majority island in the world's largest Muslim nation, famous for rice terraces and ancient temples.
hints: Between Java and Lombok in Indonesia
fun_fact: Bali has its own calendar system — the Pawukon calendar — which has a 210-day year.

name: El Capitan
prompt: A sheer 900-meter granite monolith in a California valley, the ultimate challenge for rock climbers.
fun_fact: The first free solo climb of El Capitan without ropes took Alex Honnold just 3 hours 56 minutes.

name: Danube River
prompt: Europe's second-longest river, flowing through 10 countries before emptying into the Black Sea.
hints: Flows from Germany through Austria, Hungary, and Romania
fun_fact: The Danube is the only major European river to flow from west to east, ending in the Black Sea.

name: Palau
prompt: A Pacific archipelago with a jellyfish-filled lake and some of the most biodiverse coral reefs on Earth.
fun_fact: Palau's Jellyfish Lake has millions of golden jellyfish that have evolved to be stingless over thousands of years.

name: Krakatoa
prompt: A volcanic island that exploded in 1883 with one of the loudest sounds ever recorded, heard 5,000km away.
hints: In the Sunda Strait, between Java and Sumatra
fun_fact: The 1883 Krakatoa eruption lowered global temperatures by 1.2°C for five years due to ash in the atmosphere.

name: Bhutan
prompt: A Himalayan kingdom that measures national success in Gross National Happiness rather than GDP.
fun_fact: Bhutan is the only country in the world that absorbs more carbon dioxide than it produces.

name: Madagascar
prompt: The world's fourth-largest island, separated from Africa 88 million years ago, with wildlife found nowhere else.
hints: Off the eastern coast of Africa
East of Mozambique in the Indian Ocean
fun_fact: 90% of Madagascar's wildlife is found nowhere else on Earth — it effectively evolved in complete isolation.

name: Sri Lanka
prompt: A teardrop-shaped island off the southern tip of India, famous for tea, ancient ruins, and wild elephants.
hints: Just south of the tip of India
fun_fact: Sri Lanka has the highest density of leopards in the world — Yala National Park has the most concentrated leopard population on Earth.

name: Saharan sand dunes — Erg Chebbi
prompt: Towering rust-colored sand dunes at the edge of the Sahara in North Africa, rising 150 meters.
fun_fact: The sand dunes of Erg Chebbi 'sing' — wind passing over the grains creates a low resonant hum.

name: Lake Chad
prompt: A shallow freshwater lake in central Africa that has shrunk by 90% since the 1960s.
hints: Where Chad, Niger, Nigeria, and Cameroon meet
fun_fact: Lake Chad was once one of the largest lakes in the world — it's now less than a tenth of its 1960s size.

name: Yucatan Peninsula
prompt: A flat limestone shelf jutting into the Gulf of Mexico, home to Mayan ruins and cenote sinkholes.
hints: The peninsula between the Gulf of Mexico and the Caribbean Sea
fun_fact: The meteor that killed the dinosaurs 66 million years ago struck near the tip of the Yucatan Peninsula.

name: Tuvalu
prompt: One of the world's smallest and lowest-lying nations, a Pacific atoll expected to be uninhabitable by 2050.
hints: Located in the South Pacific Ocean
fun_fact: Tuvalu's government has purchased land in Fiji as a contingency plan for when their islands become uninhabitable.

name: Mesopotamia — Tigris-Euphrates
prompt: The cradle of civilization, where two great rivers gave rise to humanity's first cities and writing.
hints: Between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers in Iraq
fun_fact: Writing was invented in Mesopotamia around 3200 BCE — the first texts were accounting records, not literature.

name: Mekong River
prompt: Southeast Asia's longest river, flowing from the Tibetan Plateau through six countries to the South China Sea.
hints: Flows through Laos, Thailand, Cambodia, and Vietnam
fun_fact: The Mekong is home to the giant freshwater stingray, the world's largest freshwater fish at up to 600 kg.

name: Ayers Rock — Uluru
prompt: A massive red sandstone formation sacred to the Anangu Aboriginal people in the Australian outback.
hints: In the Northern Territory, in central Australia
fun_fact: Uluru's color changes dramatically throughout the day — from orange to deep red to purple at sunset.

name: Yosemite Valley
prompt: A glacially carved granite valley in California, famous for its soaring cliffs and thundering waterfalls.
hints: In the Sierra Nevada Mountains of California
fun_fact: Yosemite Falls drops 739 meters, making it one of the tallest waterfalls in North America.

name: Bruges
prompt: A perfectly preserved medieval Flemish city of canals and Gothic architecture, frozen in the 14th century.
hints: In Belgium, near the North Sea coast
fun_fact: Bruges was once one of the wealthiest cities in the world — it pioneered modern banking and the stock exchange in the 14th century.

name: Patagonian Ice Fields
prompt: The largest temperate ice mass in the Southern Hemisphere outside Antarctica.
hints: In the Andes, shared by Chile and Argentina
fun_fact: The Patagonian ice fields are retreating faster than almost any glacier system outside the polar regions.

name: Galápagos Marine Reserve
prompt: One of the world's largest marine reserves, where penguins, iguanas, and sharks share the same waters.
hints: About 1,000km off the coast of Ecuador
fun_fact: The Galápagos Islands are one of the few places on Earth where you can see penguins living on the equator.

name: Lalibela
prompt: A holy city of 11 medieval churches carved entirely out of solid volcanic rock, deep in the Ethiopian highlands.
hints: In the highlands of northern Ethiopia
fun_fact: Legend says angels helped build the Lalibela churches overnight — in reality they took over 24 years to carve.

name: Yangtze River
prompt: Asia's longest river, flowing from the Tibetan Plateau to the East China Sea through the world's most populous country.
hints: Flows through central China, past Shanghai
fun_fact: The Three Gorges Dam on the Yangtze is the world's largest hydroelectric plant — it slightly slows Earth's rotation.

name: Transylvania
prompt: A forested plateau in the Carpathian Mountains of Eastern Europe, immortalized in Gothic fiction.
fun_fact: Transylvania means 'Beyond the Forest' in Latin — it's actually one of the best-preserved medieval landscapes in Europe.

name: Nazca Lines
prompt: Enormous geoglyphs etched into a desert plateau, depicting animals and shapes only visible from the air.
hints: In the coastal desert of southern Peru
fun_fact: The Nazca Lines were created between 500 BCE and 500 CE — and the ground has been so dry that they've been preserved ever since.

name: Bay of Bengal
prompt: The world's largest bay, whose warm waters fuel devastating cyclones that strike South Asia.
fun_fact: The Bay of Bengal receives 10% of all the freshwater runoff in the world from Asian rivers.

name: Mount Olympus
prompt: Greece's highest mountain and home of the gods in ancient mythology.
hints: In northern Greece, near the Aegean coast
fun_fact: Mount Olympus was first climbed in 1913 — the gods' home wasn't conquered until the 20th century.

name: Aoraki / Mount Cook
prompt: New Zealand's highest peak, a glaciated mountain in the Southern Alps.
hints: On the South Island of New Zealand
fun_fact: Edmund Hillary trained on the slopes of Aoraki/Mount Cook before his successful ascent of Everest.

name: Namib Sand Sea
prompt: A coastal sand sea where the world's tallest dunes, tinted red by iron oxide, meet the cold Atlantic.
hints: On the Atlantic coast of Namibia
fun_fact: The dunes of the Namib Sand Sea are thought to be the oldest dunes on Earth — up to 5 million years old.

name: Rhodes
prompt: A Greek island in the Aegean Sea that once harbored one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.
hints: In the Dodecanese island group of Greece
fun_fact: The Colossus of Rhodes, a giant bronze statue that once stood at its harbor entrance, was destroyed by an earthquake in 226 BCE.

name: Loire Valley
prompt: A UNESCO river valley in France dotted with hundreds of Renaissance châteaux and medieval fortresses.
hints: In central France, south of Paris
fun_fact: The Loire Valley was the playground of French kings — at one point it had over 300 châteaux within 200km.

name: Carpathian Mountains
prompt: A horseshoe-shaped mountain range in Eastern Europe, Europe's last great wilderness.
fun_fact: The Carpathians are home to the largest brown bear and wolf populations in Europe outside Russia.

name: Kathmandu
prompt: A valley city ringed by Himalayan peaks, gateway to the world's highest mountains and ancient Hindu temples.
hints: In the landlocked country between India and China
fun_fact: Kathmandu Valley contains seven UNESCO World Heritage Sites within a few square kilometers.

name: Fiordland
prompt: A remote UNESCO wilderness in the southwest of New Zealand's South Island, carved by glaciers into dramatic fjords.
hints: On the South Island of New Zealand
fun_fact: Fiordland receives up to 8,000mm of rain per year — one of the wettest places on Earth.

name: Skeleton Bay
prompt: One of the world's longest and most powerful surf breaks, a 17km sand-bottom wave in a remote desert bay.
hints: On the Atlantic coast of Namibia
fun_fact: Skeleton Bay's wave breaks for up to 17km — surfers use jet skis to return to the starting point after a single ride.

name: Hong Kong
prompt: A vertical city of skyscrapers packed onto a harbor between mountains and sea, a former British colony.
hints: On the Pearl River Delta, next to Macau
fun_fact: Hong Kong has more skyscrapers than any other city on Earth — over 550 buildings taller than 150 meters.

name: Blue Hole — Dahab
prompt: A 130-meter deep underwater sinkhole in the Red Sea, notorious as one of the world's most dangerous dive sites.
hints: In the Sinai Peninsula of Egypt
fun_fact: The Blue Hole has claimed the lives of over 200 divers, earning it the nickname 'The Diver's Cemetery.'

name: Samarkand
prompt: A Silk Road oasis city of turquoise domes, once the capital of Timur's empire and a crossroads of civilizations.
hints: In the Zeravshan Valley in Central Asia
fun_fact: Samarkand is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on Earth — over 2,750 years old.

name: Lofoten Islands
prompt: A chain of dramatic Arctic islands north of the Arctic Circle, famous for the Northern Lights and cod fishing.
hints: Above the Arctic Circle in northern Norway
fun_fact: The Lofoten Islands have the world's largest deep-water coral reef — over 40km long.

name: Pico de Orizaba
prompt: North America's third-highest peak, a massive stratovolcano still covered in glaciers in tropical Mexico.
hints: In eastern Mexico, between Puebla and Veracruz
fun_fact: Pico de Orizaba was the site of the first ascent of any major Mexican peak — by American climbers in 1848.

name: Galilee
prompt: A fertile highland region in the northern part of the Holy Land, surrounding the region's largest lake.
fun_fact: The Sea of Galilee is the lowest freshwater lake on Earth, sitting 209 meters below sea level.

name: Tagus River
prompt: The longest river on the Iberian Peninsula, flowing west to the Atlantic through Lisbon.
hints: Flows through Spain and Portugal to the Atlantic
fun_fact: Lisbon was built on seven hills at the Tagus estuary — its harbor is why Portugal became a great maritime empire.

name: Danakil Depression
prompt: One of the hottest, lowest, and most geologically active places on Earth, with lava lakes and acid pools.
hints: In the Afar Triangle in Ethiopia
fun_fact: The Danakil Depression is one of the candidates for where life first originated on Earth, due to its extreme chemical conditions.
